title: Working Tree, Staging Area, and History
summary: Follow one file from an ordinary edit through the staging area into a committed snapshot.

sketchBlocks.title: The Working Tree Explained
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The working tree is the directory where you make changes to your files. It's the first area where your files reside before they are staged or committed. When you edit a file, it changes in the working tree. 

Worked example: Suppose you have a file named `notes.txt` in your project directory. You open it and add a new line of text. This change is now in the working tree. To see the status of your working tree, you can use the command `git status`. This command will show you which files have been modified but not yet staged for commit.

sketchBlocks.title: What is the Staging Area?
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The staging area, also known as the index, is where you prepare your changes before committing them. It's like a clipboard where you gather all the changes you want to include in your next commit.

Worked example: After modifying `notes.txt`, you decide to stage it. You use the command `git add notes.txt`. This moves the changes from the working tree to the staging area. Now, if you run `git status`, it will show `notes.txt` as staged for commit.

sketchBlocks.title: How to Commit Changes
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Committing is the process of saving your staged changes to the repository's history. Each commit is a snapshot of your project at a point in time.

Worked example: With `notes.txt` staged, you commit the changes using `git commit -m "Add new line to notes"`. This command creates a new commit in the repository's history. You can view the commit history with `git log --oneline`, which will show a list of commits, including your latest one.

sketchBlocks.title: Exploring the Commit History
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The commit history is a record of all the changes made to the repository. It allows you to see what changes were made, when, and by whom.

Worked example: To view the commit history, use `git log --oneline`. This command provides a concise view of the commit history, showing each commit's hash and message. You can also use `git show` to see detailed information about a specific commit, including the changes made.

quizDraft.title: Track Changes in the Working Tree
quizDraft.prompt: You have a file named `project.txt` in your working directory. Modify the file by adding a line of text, then check the status of your working tree to see the changes.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Edit the file and use `git status` to check changes.

help.concept: The working tree is where you make changes to files before staging them.
help.hint_2: Add a line to `project.txt` and run `git status` to see the changes.

quizDraft.title: Stage Changes for Commit
quizDraft.prompt: You have modified `project.txt`. Stage this file for commit and verify its status in the staging area.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git add` to stage the file.

help.concept: The staging area holds changes before they are committed.
help.hint_1: Use `git add project.txt` to stage the file.
help.hint_2: After staging, use `git status` to verify the file is staged.

quizDraft.title: Commit Staged Changes
quizDraft.prompt: Commit the staged changes in `project.txt` with a message 'Update project file'. Verify the commit in the history.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.

help.concept: Committing saves staged changes to the repository history.

quizDraft.title: Explore Commit History
quizDraft.prompt: View the commit history to see the latest changes. Use a command that provides a concise view of the history.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git log --oneline` to view the history.

help.concept: The commit history shows all changes made to the repository.
help.hint_1: Use `git log --oneline` for a concise history.
help.hint_2: This command shows commit hashes and messages.

quizDraft.title: Identify the Working Tree
quizDraft.prompt: Which area of a Git repository is directly modified when you edit a file?
quizDraft.hint: Think about where changes first appear before staging.

help.concept: The working tree is where files are modified before staging.
help.hint_1: The working tree is the first area affected by file edits.
help.hint_2: Changes appear in the working tree before being staged.

quizDraft.title: Purpose of the Staging Area
quizDraft.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the staging area in Git?
quizDraft.hint: Consider what happens to changes before they are committed.

help.concept: The staging area prepares changes for commit.
help.hint_1: The staging area holds changes before committing.
help.hint_2: It's like a clipboard for changes to be committed.

quizDraft.title: Command to Stage Files
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the blank: To stage a file named `example.txt`, use the command `[blank1] example.txt`.
quizDraft.template: To stage a file named `example.txt`, use the command `[blank1] example.txt`.
quizDraft.hint: The command adds files to the staging area.

help.concept: `the missing term` stages files for commit.
help.hint_2: Use `the missing term` to stage files.

quizDraft.title: Viewing Commit History
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the blank: To view a concise commit history, use the command `[blank1]`.
quizDraft.template: To view a concise commit history, use the command `[blank1]`.
quizDraft.hint: This command shows a brief list of commits.

help.concept: `the missing term` shows a concise commit history.

quizDraft.title: Areas of a Git Repository
quizDraft.prompt: Which of the following are areas of a Git repository? Select all that apply.
quizDraft.hint: Think about the main components of a Git repository.

help.concept: A Git repository consists of the working tree, staging area, and commit history.
help.hint_1: Consider where changes are made, staged, and committed.
help.hint_2: The repository includes areas for editing, staging, and history.

quizDraft.title: Order of Operations in Git
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the following operations in the correct order for tracking changes in Git.
quizDraft.hint: Consider the sequence from editing to committing.

help.concept: The correct order is editing, staging, then committing.
